medications.indication_rationale: Patient is vomiting and should remain nil by mouth in preparation for likely hospital assessment/surgery; oral medication is also unlikely to be tolerated or effective.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected surgical abdomen requiring NBM status

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all oral intake of food and fluids (nil by mouth) until assessed in the Emergency Department.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prepares for possible imaging and surgery, and reduces gallbladder stimulation and vomiting.

patient_education_points.point: This sounds different from your usual gallstone pain — the fact that it hasn't settled after many hours and you have a fever suggests your gallbladder has become infected and inflamed (acute cholecystitis).
You need to go to the Emergency Department today for an ultrasound, blood tests, and likely antibiotics through a drip — this shouldn't wait for a routine GP appointment.
Don't eat or drink anything until you've been seen, in case you need surgery.
If the pain suddenly disappears completely but you then feel extremely unwell with a swollen, tender belly, call 000 immediately, as this could mean the gallbladder has ruptured.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Ultrasound findings, inflammatory markers, response to IV antibiotics, and timing of surgical cholecystectomy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Sudden complete resolution of pain followed by worsening general unwellness and abdominal swelling — call 000 immediately, as this may indicate gallbladder perforation.

must_include_items: Explanation that this differs from her usual self-limiting biliary colic due to duration and fever
Safety-netting for signs of gallbladder perforation
acceptable_alternatives: Urgent in-person GP or urgent care assessment with same-day escalation pathway to ED if imaging/surgical facilities unavailable locally
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient this is just her usual gallstone pain
Recommending oral antiemetics/analgesia as sufficient management
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Failing to escalate to same-day in-person/ED assessment given the fever and prolonged duration is a significant safety failure.
